region_of_origin: Andhra Pradesh / Telangana, India (served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken 65 or Andhra-style deep-fried chicken with spicy gravy. FOODON_03315091 (chicken dish) used as the closest parent class. An exact match is not possible as this specific South Indian preparation is not represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chicken 65 or deep-fried chicken in spicy Andhra-style gravy. A0CKM (chicken meat, cooked) used as the closest defensible parent anchor; preparation method (deep-frying followed by sautéing in tomato-chilli gravy) and coating (cornflour/maida) are noted separately as they are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chicken 65 or equivalent South Indian fried chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Andhra Chicken 65 weighs approximately 300 g, comprising deep-fried boneless chicken pieces sautéed in spicy Andhra-style gravy.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken (cooked, spiced) and fried chicken composite estimate, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05064
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Andhra Chicken 65 is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked spiced chicken and fried chicken entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, fried' (NDB 05064) and adjusted for gravy components (tomato, chilli, oil). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with ICMR-NIN/USDA ranges. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Andhra Chicken 65. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous mixed fried chicken dish with cornflour/maida coating and tomato-based gravy. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is categorised as low. The carbohydrate contribution (10 g/100 g) comes primarily from cornflour coating and tomato gravy. Cornflour has a GI of approximately 85 (high) but is substantially diluted by the dominant protein and fat matrix of the dish, yielding a blended low-to-medium boundary GI estimate of 55. GL per 300 g serving = (55 × 30 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 16.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers 1200 mg sodium, representing approximately 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced salt in the marinade and gravy to bring per-serving sodium below 600 mg; avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (10 g/100 g, GI 55 — low boundary) from cornflour coating and tomato gravy; high protein (15 g/100 g) partially attenuates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (150–200 g) of Andhra Chicken 65 provided it is not paired with high-GI staples such as white rice or naan; requesting a reduced-coating or grilled preparation further lowers the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 13.3 g/100 g with saturated fat 3.3 g/100 g and cholesterol 60 mg/100 g; deep-frying also generates trans fats (0.17 g/100 g) and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100–150 g) and prefer a grilled or baked preparation to reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and AGE exposure; the high sodium content also warrants caution given its contribution to blood pressure elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; dish also contains nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, green chillies).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried preparation and request a grilled or baked version; those with nightshade sensitivity should also request removal of tomatoes and green chillies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Andhra Chicken 65 is a South Indian deep-fried chicken dish originating from Andhra Pradesh and Telangana, India, and widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. The dish begins with boneless chicken pieces marinated in yogurt, red chilli powder, turmeric, cumin, ginger, garlic, and cornflour or maida, then deep-fried until crisp and golden. In the Hyderabadi or Andhra gravy version, the fried chicken is subsequently sautéed in a rich, spicy, tangy gravy made from tomatoes, green chillies, curry leaves, and aromatic spices. The result is a dish with a crispy exterior softened by the gravy coating, delivering intense heat, tanginess, and deep umami from the spice blend. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 216 kcal, 15 g protein, 13.3 g fat, and 10 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 400 mg — making it a high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate dish with elevated sodium. The deep-frying step generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) relevant to inflammatory conditions. Clinically, the dish is high-concern for hypertension due to sodium load, moderate-concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the cornflour coating and GI of 55 (low boundary), and moderate-concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and trans fat from frying. It is halal-certified in most Singapore restaurant contexts. Common pairings include plain rice, roti, or as a standalone starter.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Andhra Chicken 65?
What is the difference between Chicken 65 and Andhra Chicken 65?
Is Chicken 65 good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Andhra Chicken 65 is not available as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked spiced chicken and USDA SR Legacy fried chicken entries, adjusted for gravy components. Input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these approved sources and retained where consistent. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Andhra Chicken 65 (deep-fried with spicy gravy) is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy composite for fried spiced chicken with tomato gravy. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite South Indian fried chicken dish with gravy are not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (cooked spiced chicken) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05064 (chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, fried), adjusted for gravy components. Input record provenance is ai_generated with confidence 0.7; values were retained where consistent with approved source ranges. GI of 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney GI Database boundary (GI ≤55 = low); this is an analogous estimate based on similar battered/coated fried chicken dishes; no direct GI measurement for Andhra Chicken 65 exists in the approved GI database. GL per 300 g serving calculated as (55 × 30 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 16.5. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2, as these are the closest parent class anchors rather than exact matches. The dish is served at Curry Village – Authentic North Indian Cuisine, Boat Quay, Singapore, and is listed on GrabFood. Cultural tags corrected: primary cuisine is South Indian / Hyderabadi (Andhra Pradesh origin), not North Indian, despite the restaurant branding.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' — 'broader' is not a valid value per the validator schema; allowed values are ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' — 'adapted' is not a valid value per the validator schema; allowed values are ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — per University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is categorised as low, not medium. GI=55 sits exactly at the low/medium boundary and the University of Sydney database places it in the low category.
Iteration 3: Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to clarify the low classification rationale.
Iteration 3: Updated notes field to document the gi_category correction and ontology match_type corrections.
